title: ACAS Guide: Workplace Policies and Procedures
topicLabel: Essential workplace policies every employer needs
chapters: Non-statutory guidance · Published by ACAS · Covers the essential employment policies every employer should have, contractual versus non-contractual policies, and the legal framework for workplace rules
desc: The ACAS guide on workplace policies covers the essential policies that employers should have in place, the legal distinction between contractual and non-contractual policies, and the role of policies in demonstrating procedural fairness. While there is no single statute requiring employers to have a comprehensive policy handbook, various statutes require specific policies or procedures. ERA 1996 s.1 requires a written statement of employment particulars including disciplinary and grievance procedures. The Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 s.2(3) requires a written health and safety policy (for employers with 5+ employees). The Equality Act 2010 creates an expectation that employers will have equal opportunities and anti-harassment policies. The guide covers the distinction between contractual policies (which form part of the contract of employment and cannot be changed unilaterally) and non-contractual policies (which the employer can change by giving reasonable notice). The guide also covers policy communication, training, review, and the importance of consistency in application.
legalStatus: Non-statutory guidance. However, the existence and quality of workplace policies is relevant to the reasonableness assessment in unfair dismissal cases and to the employer's defence under EqA 2010 s.109(4) ('all reasonable steps' defence to vicarious liability for discrimination by employees).

sections.title: Core policies every employer should have
sections.summary: ACAS recommends that every employer should have the following core policies: a disciplinary procedure (required by ERA 1996 s.1 — must be referenced in written statement of particulars), a grievance procedure (ERA 1996 s.1), an equal opportunities and diversity policy (supports EqA 2010 compliance and the 'all reasonable steps' defence), an anti-bullying and harassment policy (supports EqA 2010 s.109(4) defence and the new preventive duty under s.40A), a health and safety policy (mandatory for 5+ employees under HSWA 1974 s.2(3)), an absence management policy, a data protection and privacy policy (supports UK GDPR compliance), a whistleblowing policy (supports PIDA 1998 framework), a flexible working policy (supports ERA 1996 ss.80F–80I), and a social media and IT use policy. Larger employers should also consider policies on dress code, alcohol and drugs, expenses, maternity and family leave, and redundancy.
sections.text:
ACAS recommends the following core employment policies:

Statutorily Required:
• Disciplinary procedure — must be referenced in the written statement of employment particulars (ERA 1996 s.1)
• Grievance procedure — must be referenced in the written statement (ERA 1996 s.1)
• Health and safety policy — employers with 5 or more employees must have a written policy (HSWA 1974 s.2(3))

Strongly Recommended:
• Equal opportunities and diversity policy — supports the 'all reasonable steps' defence under EqA 2010 s.109(4)
• Anti-bullying and harassment policy — supports EqA 2010 compliance and the new s.40A preventive duty for sexual harassment
• Whistleblowing policy — provides the framework for protected disclosures under PIDA 1998
• Data protection and privacy policy — supports UK GDPR compliance and demonstrates data protection by design
• Absence management policy — provides the framework for fair and consistent absence management
• Flexible working policy — supports compliance with ERA 1996 ss.80F–80I and the ACAS Code of Practice 5
• Social media and IT use policy — defines acceptable use of company and personal technology
• Maternity, paternity, adoption, shared parental, and parental bereavement leave policies

Additional policies for larger employers:
• Redundancy policy and procedure
• Dress code and appearance
• Alcohol, drugs, and substance misuse
• Expenses and travel
• Homeworking and hybrid working
• Training and development

leadingCases.name: Canniffe v East Riding of Yorkshire Council [2000] IRLR 555
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that for the 'all reasonable steps' defence under the discrimination legislation, the employer must show that it took steps to prevent the particular act complained of and to prevent employees from doing acts of that description. Having a policy is a relevant step but is not sufficient alone — the policy must be communicated, enforced, and staff must be trained on it.
leadingCases.significance: Established that a policy alone is insufficient for the 'all reasonable steps' defence — communication, training, and enforcement are required

leadingCases.name: Allay (UK) Ltd v Gehlen [2021] ICR 645
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that stale and ineffective training is not a reasonable step for the purposes of the employer's defence. Where equality and diversity training was delivered two years previously and harassment continued, the employer could not rely on the training as evidence of having taken all reasonable steps. Training must be refreshed and effective.
leadingCases.significance: Key recent authority — stale training is not sufficient for the 'all reasonable steps' defence; training must be current and effective

refs: ERA 1996 s.1 (written statement of employment particulars — must include disciplinary and grievance procedures)
HSWA 1974 s.2(3) (written health and safety policy — 5+ employees)
EqA 2010 s.109(4) ('all reasonable steps' defence to employer vicarious liability)
EqA 2010 s.40A (duty to prevent sexual harassment — from 26 October 2024)
UK GDPR Art.5(2) and Art.24 (accountability principle — requires documented policies and procedures)
PIDA 1998 / ERA 1996 Part IVA (whistleblowing framework — policy should facilitate disclosures)
ACAS Code of Practice 1 (Disciplinary and Grievance — foundation of the disciplinary policy)
ACAS Code of Practice 5 (Flexible Working — foundation of the flexible working policy)

sections.title: Legal status of policies, incorporation, and unilateral change
sections.summary: The legal distinction between contractual and non-contractual workplace policies is critical. A contractual policy (or one that has been incorporated into the contract of employment) forms part of the employee's terms and conditions and cannot be changed without the employee's consent. A non-contractual policy can be changed by the employer giving reasonable notice. Whether a policy is contractual depends on the intention of the parties, the language used (e.g. 'the company reserves the right to amend this policy'), whether the policy is referred to in the contract of employment, and the surrounding circumstances. Employers should be explicit about whether policies are contractual or non-contractual. Unilateral changes to contractual terms (including contractual policies) may constitute a fundamental breach of contract, entitling the employee to resign and claim constructive unfair dismissal. The employer should negotiate and consult before changing any policy, regardless of its legal status.
sections.text:
Whether a workplace policy is contractual depends on:

• the language of the policy itself: does it use mandatory language ('the employer will') or permissive language ('the employer may')?
• whether the policy is expressly referred to in the contract of employment as a contractual term
• whether there is a clear statement that the policy is non-contractual and may be amended
• the conduct of the parties: has the policy been consistently applied as if it were contractual?

Contractual policies:
• cannot be changed without the employee's consent
• unilateral change may constitute a fundamental breach of contract
• the employee may claim constructive unfair dismissal if the change is imposed
• the employer may need to dismiss and re-engage on new terms (with all the risks that entails)

Non-contractual policies:
• can be changed by the employer giving reasonable notice
• the employer should still consult before making changes
• employees should be informed of changes and given access to the updated policy

Best practice: every policy should include a clear statement of whether it is contractual or non-contractual, and should include a reservation of the right to amend (if non-contractual).

leadingCases.name: Bateman v Asda Stores Ltd [2010] IRLR 370
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT considered whether an employer's handbook policy was contractual or non-contractual. The EAT held that the question is one of construction — the tribunal must look at the language of the policy, the context in which it was issued, and whether the contract of employment expressly incorporates it. A policy described as 'non-contractual' but expressed in mandatory terms may still be found to be contractual.
leadingCases.significance: Key authority on the construction approach to determining contractual status of workplace policies

leadingCases.name: Keeley v Fosroc International Ltd [2006] EWCA Civ 1277
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the terms of an enhanced redundancy scheme contained in a staff handbook were incorporated into the employee's contract of employment. The fact that the scheme was contained in a handbook (rather than the contract itself) did not prevent incorporation where the contract expressly referred to the handbook terms.
leadingCases.significance: Leading authority on incorporation of handbook terms into the contract of employment

refs: ERA 1996 s.1–7B (written statement of employment particulars)
ERA 1996 s.4 (statement of changes to employment particulars)
ERA 1996 s.95(1)(c) (constructive dismissal — employee resigns in response to employer's breach)
Western Excavating (ECC) Ltd v Sharp [1978] ICR 221 (constructive dismissal test — repudiatory breach of contract)

pendingAmendments: ERA 2025 s.131 — fire and rehire provisions interact with contractual/non-contractual policy distinction
EqA 2010 s.40A preventive duty — requires review of harassment policies

parliamentaryOrigin.statutoryBasis: Non-statutory guidance. However, the existence, communication, and enforcement of workplace policies is directly relevant to: (1) the reasonableness assessment in unfair dismissal (ERA 1996 s.98(4)); (2) the 'all reasonable steps' defence to vicarious liability for discrimination (EqA 2010 s.109(4)); (3) the preventive duty for sexual harassment (EqA 2010 s.40A); and (4) the contractual framework for employment.
parliamentaryOrigin.note: The ACAS guide on workplace policies is foundational for all employer compliance. The distinction between contractual and non-contractual policies determines whether terms can be changed unilaterally — a question at the heart of the fire-and-rehire provisions in ERA 2025 s.131.

precedingDocuments.title: ERA 1996 s.1-7B
precedingDocuments.significance: Written statement of employment particulars must reference disciplinary and grievance procedures. Updated from April 2020 to require more detailed particulars from day one.

precedingDocuments.title: EqA 2010 s.109(4)
precedingDocuments.significance: 'All reasonable steps' defence — having, communicating, and enforcing policies is a core element.

leadingCases.name: Canniffe v East Riding of Yorkshire Council [2000] IRLR 555
leadingCases.heldText: A policy alone is insufficient for the 'all reasonable steps' defence — communication, training, and enforcement required.

leadingCases.name: Allay (UK) v Gehlen [2021] ICR 645
leadingCases.heldText: Stale training is not sufficient — training must be current and effective.
leadingCases.significance: Stale training fails the 'all reasonable steps' defence.

leadingCases.name: Keeley v Fosroc International [2006] EWCA Civ 1277
leadingCases.heldText: Handbook terms can be incorporated into the contract of employment where the contract expressly refers to them.
leadingCases.significance: Incorporation of handbook terms into contract.

leadingCases.name: Bateman v Asda Stores [2010] IRLR 370
leadingCases.heldText: A policy described as 'non-contractual' but expressed in mandatory terms may still be contractual — the question is one of construction.
leadingCases.significance: Construction approach to contractual status of policies.
